name: Mathematics-I
description: Calculus, linear algebra, and basic discrete tools for IT applications.

theory: Limits describe how a function behaves as its input approaches a point. For IT students, limits underpin numerical stability and algorithmic approximations.
Continuity ensures small changes in input yield small changes in output. Many data-processing functions assume continuity when approximating values or smoothing data.
Standard trigonometric and exponential limits are building blocks used across calculus-based proofs and derivations in computing contexts.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A function f(x) has limit L as x→a if values of f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L for x sufficiently near a.

keyPoints: Continuity requires left and right limits equal function value
Use algebraic simplification to resolve indeterminate forms
Limits support analysis of asymptotic processes

examples.solution: Use series expansion or L'Hôpital's rule to obtain 1/2.
Both side limits equal 0, function value 0; continuous.

theory: Derivative measures instantaneous rate of change and is central to modelling trends and optimization in IT tasks such as performance tuning.
Product, quotient and chain rules enable differentiation of composed expressions that occur frequently in algorithmic cost models.
Applications include maxima/minima problems used in resource allocation and initial sensitivity estimates for numerical methods.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Derivative f'(a) = lim(h->0) [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h when the limit exists.

examples.solution: Use product rule to obtain e^x(x^2+2x)
Differentiate both sides wrt time and substitute data.

notes: Label units when solving applied problems

examQuestions.question: Solve a constrained optimization using derivatives.

theory: Taylor series express functions as infinite polynomials centered around a point; truncations provide practical approximations.
Remainder terms guide error estimation, which is vital when approximating functions in numerical algorithms.
Common expansions like e^x, sin x and cos x are used to linearize models in engineering and compute small argument values efficiently.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Taylor series of f about a is sum_{n=0}^∞ f^{(n)}(a)/n! (x−a)^n when convergent.

keyPoints: Truncation error depends on next derivative

examQuestions.question: Maclaurin series for sin x up to x^3.
Estimate error for a 2nd order approximation.

theory: Matrices represent linear maps between vector spaces and are essential in graphics, machine learning, and systems of equations.
Determinants indicate volume scaling, invertibility, and appear in solution formulas for linear systems.
Rank and null space classification determine solvability and degrees of freedom for linear models used in data fitting.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Determinant is a scalar value that can be computed from a square matrix and encodes invertibility and volume scaling.

examples.solution: Use matrix inversion or Cramer's rule to obtain x,y.

pyqs.question: Compute determinant of a simple matrix

references: Linear Algebra and Its Applications - Gilbert Strang

theory: Solving linear systems is essential in modelling and simulation; Gaussian elimination transforms a system into triangular form for back-substitution.
LU decomposition factors a matrix into lower and upper triangular forms, enabling efficient repeated solves as in iterative algorithms and parameter sweeps.
Consistency depends on rank relations between coefficient and augmented matrices; singular systems require special handling.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A system Ax=b is consistent if b lies in the column space of A; otherwise no solution exists.

keyPoints: Singular systems have infinite or no solutions

examples.solution: For multiple RHS solves where factorization cost amortizes.

notes: Be aware of floating point rounding

theory: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors characterize linear transformations by invariant directions and scaling factors, used in PCA and system dynamics.
Diagonalization simplifies powers and exponentials of matrices, which is useful in solving linear recurrences and stability analysis.
Computational methods like QR algorithm approximate eigenpairs for large matrices common in data analysis tasks.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: An eigenvector v of A satisfies Av = λv where λ is the associated eigenvalue.

examples.solution: Eigenvectors of covariance indicate principal directions.

notes: Normalize eigenvectors when used for projection

theory: Probability assigns measures to events and underlies statistical inference and randomized algorithms used in IT.
Conditional probability and Bayes' theorem enable updating beliefs based on observed evidence, crucial in troubleshooting and classification tasks.
Discrete distributions like binomial and Poisson model counting processes common in systems and queuing analyses.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Probability P(E) quantifies the likelihood of event E occurring and satisfies 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1 and P(S)=1 for sample space S.

examples.solution: Combine prior and evidence for posterior probability.

examQuestions.question: Apply Bayes to a medical diagnostic example.

references: A First Course in Probability - Ross

theory: Random variables map outcomes to numeric values used in modelling and algorithms, with PMF/PDF and CDF describing their distribution.
Expectation and variance summarize central tendency and spread—used when designing algorithms that rely on average-case performance.
Discrete and continuous distributions provide templates; sampling from these distributions supports simulation tasks.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A random variable is a function from the sample space to the real numbers; its distribution captures probabilities of outcomes.

formulas.name: Expectation
formulas.formula: E[X]=Σ x P(X=x) or ∫ x f(x) dx

examples.solution: Use λ to model average rate.

notes: Use CLT when sample size large

theory: Estimation methods produce point and interval estimates for population parameters from sample data—key in analytics and A/B testing.
Confidence intervals quantify uncertainty around estimates and guide decision thresholds for systems and experiments.
Sampling techniques like stratified and random sampling affect estimator bias and variance and are important in data collection design.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A point estimator maps sample data to an estimate of a population parameter; interval estimators provide a range with an associated confidence level.

examples.problem: Compute 95% CI for mean given s and n

examples.solution: Choose stratified sampling to reduce variance.

notes: Include sample size effect in conclusions

examQuestions.question: Design a sampling scheme for survey.

theory: Numerical root-finding methods locate zeros of functions when analytic solutions are impractical. Newton-Raphson offers fast local convergence but needs derivative and good initial guess.
Interpolation constructs approximate functions that pass through sample points; Lagrange form is explicit but can be unstable for many nodes.
These methods support computational tasks in engineering, simulation, and algorithm prototyping within IT projects.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Numerical methods approximate solutions to continuous problems using discrete computations designed for accuracy and stability.

keyPoints: Prefer stable forms for many nodes

theory: When analytic integration is difficult, numerical quadrature uses weighted sums to approximate definite integrals with known error bounds.
Finite difference approximations estimate derivatives from discrete data and are common in signal processing and numerical optimization.
Understanding error terms and step size selection is key to reliable computational results in IT applications.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Numerical integration approximates ∫ f(x) dx via weighted sums computed on discrete nodes.

keyPoints: Simpson more accurate than trapezoid for smooth functions
Error term depends on derivative order
Use adaptive quadrature for irregular integrands

examples.solution: Use Simpson with n=2 for a reasonable estimate.

notes: Refine step size for better accuracy

theory: Complexity analysis categorizes algorithms by resource growth; Big-O abstracts away constant factors and lower order terms to compare algorithms at scale.
Space complexity tracks extra memory used and is crucial when designing systems with tight memory budgets like embedded IoT devices.
Simple recurrence relations model divide-and-conquer runtime and are solved using methods such as Master theorem.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Big-O notation f(n)=O(g(n)) means there exists c,N such that f(n)≤c g(n) for all n≥N.

keyPoints: Apply complexity to choose data structures
notes: Express cost in simplest asymptotic form
